findings.summary: Pipeline stage list uses raw tuple indexing instead of a typed protocol, forcing every consumer to know the implicit (name, func[, skip]) arity
evidence: Line 183-186: `stage_name = stage_spec[0]; stage_func = stage_spec[1]; skip_condition = stage_spec[2] if len(stage_spec) > 2 else False` — positional indexing repeated in both `_execute_pipeline` and `_resume_pipeline`
Line 334-365 in `_resume_pipeline` repeats the same three-position unpack logic verbatim, including the `if len(stage_spec) > 2` guard
The tuple layout is an implicit contract not enforced by any type — adding a fourth element would silently pass and be ignored
findings.suggestion: Replace the raw tuple with a `@dataclass` or `NamedTuple` (e.g. `PipelineStage(name, func, skip=False)`) and extract the iteration + skip guard into a single helper so both `_execute_pipeline` and `_resume_pipeline` share one code path.

findings.summary: Review type dispatch in `run_llm_review_pipeline` is an explicit if/elif chain over string literals rather than a mapping, leaking the dispatch contract across two modules
evidence: pipeline_runner.py lines 137-147: four `if/elif` branches on `review_type` string to call the corresponding `generate_*` function — the same string literals appear in `generator.py` `validate_review_quality` (lines 161, 189, 223, 253, 283)
Adding a new review type requires changes in at least three places: the if/elif dispatcher, the validator, and the `REVIEW_MIN_WORDS` dict in `manuscript.py`
The `ReviewMode` class uses bare string constants (line 42-44) with no link to the per-type generator mapping, adding a third encoding of the same concept
findings.suggestion: Define a `REVIEW_GENERATORS: dict[str, Callable]` mapping at the module boundary in `generator.py` and replace the if/elif chain with a single lookup. The validator can reference the same dict to enumerate known types.

findings.summary: The partial-response-save block inside `stream_query` is copy-pasted verbatim into four separate exception branches, creating 4x maintenance surface for one piece of boundary logic
evidence: Lines 947-973 (Timeout retry path): constructs `ResponseMetadata`, calls `save_streaming_response`, catches `Exception as save_error`
Lines 978-1004 (Timeout final failure path): identical block, only `partial_response=True` and `streaming_time_seconds` differ
Lines 1024-1050 (ConnectionError retry path): third copy of the same block
Lines 1056-1082 (ConnectionError final failure path): fourth copy — the only difference from the second copy is the logger message text
findings.suggestion: Extract the save-partial-response logic into `_save_partial_streaming_response(self, full_response, save_path, prompt, model_name, chunk_count, start_time, partial)` and call it from each exception branch with the varying arguments.

findings.summary: `LLMConfig.from_env()` is called repeatedly inside `generate_review_with_metrics` per-loop iteration rather than being passed in as a parameter
evidence: Line 527: `max_tokens = LLMConfig.from_env().long_max_tokens` — creates a config object when `max_tokens` is None

Line 583: `config = LLMConfig.from_env()` — creates another config object inside the per-attempt streaming loop to read `review_timeout`, `heartbeat_interval`, `stall_threshold`, `early_warning_threshold`
Line 493: `max_length = LLMConfig.from_env().max_input_length` inside `extract_manuscript_text`
The caller `create_review_client` (line 315) already constructs a `LLMConfig` — this config is not passed down, so it is reconstructed independently at least 3 times per review generation
findings.suggestion: Thread the `LLMConfig` instance from `create_review_client` through `generate_review_with_metrics` (and `extract_manuscript_text`) as an explicit parameter rather than reconstructing it at call sites.

findings.summary: The stall detection in `stream_query` compares `current_time - last_chunk_time` but `last_chunk_time` is set to `current_time` on the same iteration, so the condition can never be true within a single chunk
evidence: Line 870: `last_chunk_time = current_time`

Lines 873-876: `if (last_chunk_time and (current_time - last_chunk_time) > self.config.stall_threshold)` — `last_chunk_time` was just assigned `current_time`, so `current_time - last_chunk_time == 0` at this point
The stall check uses the wrong variable — it should compare against the time of the *previous* chunk, which would require saving `last_chunk_time` before updating it
findings.suggestion: Save the previous chunk time before updating: `prev_chunk_time = last_chunk_time; last_chunk_time = current_time` and use `prev_chunk_time` in the stall guard, checking `prev_chunk_time is not None` as the None sentinel.

findings.summary: The 30%-timeout warning in `stream_query` has no rate-limiting guard, so it emits a log message for every single streamed chunk after the threshold is crossed
evidence: Lines 911-924: `if elapsed > self.config.timeout * 0.3` is checked inside the per-chunk `if chunk:` block with no deduplication or interval guard
For a model generating 500 tokens past the 30% mark, this emits 500 separate `INFO` log messages with almost identical content
The condition only checks `remaining > 0`, not 'has this already been logged recently'
findings.suggestion: Track a boolean `_timeout_warned` flag (or the last-warned elapsed value) outside the chunk loop and only emit the warning the first time (or at most once per 10% of timeout remaining).

findings.summary: `clean_coverage_files` prepends `**/` to patterns that already start with `**/`, producing invalid triple-star globs for recursive patterns
evidence: Lines 339-342: `glob_pattern = f"**/{pattern}" if not pattern.startswith("**/") else pattern` — the guard `not pattern.startswith("**/")` is correct, but the default patterns on lines 327-329 ALL start with `**/`
Line 342: `for file_path in repo_root.glob(glob_pattern)` — because the guard is correct, the existing defaults are passed through unchanged, which is fine

However the branch `f"**/{pattern}"` will fire for any custom caller-supplied pattern that lacks `**/` prefix, e.g. `.coverage` → `**/.coverage`, which is correct; but a caller passing e.g. `.coverage*` would get `**/.coverage*` while the docstring says 'file patterns' without stating this transformation, creating a surprising implicit rewrite
findings.suggestion: Document the auto-prefix behavior explicitly in the docstring, or normalize all patterns to be absolute glob patterns and remove the conditional prefix logic.

findings.summary: A `pass` branch in `copy_final_deliverables` silently does nothing after an existence check, making the intent unreadable
evidence: Lines 526-532: `if combined_pdf_src.exists(): pass` — the `pass` branch documents a fallback path but actually performs no action and the comment is on the `else` not the `if`
The logic flow `if exists: pass; else: reassign` is semantically equivalent to `if not exists: reassign` but reads as if the `if` branch intentionally skips something
This pattern was likely a placeholder that was never cleaned up
findings.suggestion: Replace with `if not project_specific_pdf.exists(): combined_pdf_src, combined_pdf_dst = generic_pdf, output_dir / 'project_combined.pdf'` for a direct single-branch form.

findings.summary: `generate_review_with_metrics` combines prompt rendering, retry orchestration, streaming, heartbeat monitoring, progress display, deduplication, and quality validation in a single 140-line body
evidence: Lines 513-675 (approx): the function body contains: template render (line 537), retry loop (line 546), temperature adjustment per attempt (line 551), off-topic retry prompt construction (lines 555-569), StreamingProgress lifecycle (lines 577-608), StreamHeartbeatMonitor lifecycle (lines 584-602), fallback to non-streaming query (line 612), quality validation (lines 614-616), best-response tracking (line 619), deduplication post-processing (lines 640-648), and metrics assembly

The `except Exception: response = client.query(...)` on line 612 silently swallows the streaming failure and falls back — this control-flow branch is buried inside a 3-level nesting of loops and try blocks

The function takes 9 parameters (client, text, review_type, review_name, template_class, model_name, temperature, max_tokens, max_retries) — several of these encode policy decisions that belong in a caller-level config object
findings.suggestion: Extract at minimum: (1) `_stream_with_heartbeat(client, prompt, options, review_name, config)` for the streaming+monitoring concern; (2) `_build_retry_prompt(prompt, had_off_topic)` for prompt mutation; leaving `generate_review_with_metrics` as a retry loop coordinator.

findings.summary: `_execute_pipeline` and the latter half of `_resume_pipeline` implement parallel stage-execution loops with divergent exit-code-2 handling, creating two maintained versions of the same logic
evidence: Lines 195-202 (`_execute_pipeline`): exit code 2 handled by reconstructing a `PipelineStageResult` with `success=True`

Lines 370-379 (`_resume_pipeline`): `if hasattr(result, 'exit_code') and result.exit_code == 2` — uses `hasattr` defensively on a typed dataclass, then reconstructs an identical `PipelineStageResult` with the same fields
The `hasattr` guard on a `PipelineStageResult` (which always has `exit_code`) is a design smell indicating the two loops were written independently
Both loops also independently call `self._save_checkpoint(...)` after successful stages without sharing the logic
findings.suggestion: Extract the stage-execution, exit-code-2 normalization, and checkpoint-save into `_run_single_stage(stage_spec, executed_stage_num, pipeline_start)` returning a `PipelineStageResult`, and call it from both `_execute_pipeline` and the resume loop.

findings.summary: `validate_review_quality` encodes per-review-type structural expectations (header lists, score patterns, section variations) inline as local literals, making the function 310 lines of type-keyed conditionals
evidence: Lines 161-311: five `if/elif review_type ==` blocks each containing multi-element lists of keyword variations, regex patterns, and section names as string literals
The `header_variations` list on lines 163-177 has 5 tuples each containing a list of synonyms — this is data, not logic, but is embedded in a function body
Adding a new review type requires inserting a new `elif` branch here AND in `pipeline_runner.py`'s dispatcher AND in `REVIEW_MIN_WORDS` — no single registration point
findings.suggestion: Define a `REVIEW_TYPE_SPECS: dict[str, ReviewTypeSpec]` module-level mapping where `ReviewTypeSpec` holds expected sections, min-word overrides, and score patterns. Replace the if/elif chain with a lookup into this dict.

findings.summary: `clean_output_directories` handles log archiving, selective preservation, checkpoint preservation, subdir recreation, and root-level cleanup in a single 120-line function
evidence: Lines 97-221: the function body sequentially: (1) discovers project names via directory scan (lines 117-122), (2) calls `clean_root_output_directory` (line 125), (3) defines subdirs list (lines 127-138), (4) archives log files with timestamping (lines 151-176), (5) iterates items with checkpoint preservation logic (lines 188-211), (6) recreates subdirectory structure (lines 215-217)
Log archiving (lines 151-176) is an unrelated concern to directory cleaning and has no dedicated function

The inline project-name discovery via `projects_dir.iterdir()` (lines 117-122) duplicates logic from `infrastructure/project/discovery.py` with a comment explaining why it avoids the import (circular import)
findings.suggestion: Extract `_archive_log_files(logs_dir)` for the log-archiving concern. The circular-import workaround in the comment (lines 114-116) is a sign that `clean_output_directories` should not own project discovery — pass `project_names` as a parameter instead of computing it internally.
